title: Emergency Guide: What to Do if Your Dog Swallowed a Hearing Aid Battery
meta_desc: If your dog ate a hearing aid battery, act now. This high-priority emergency requires immediate veterinary care to prevent internal chemical burns.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swallowing a hearing aid battery is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that requires immediate veterinary care to prevent severe internal chemical and electrical burns.

What are hearing aid batteries and why are they dangerous for dogs?

  • Hearing aid batteries are typically small, disc-shaped button cell batteries.
  • Despite their size, they contain concentrated chemicals and can generate an electrical current.
  • They cause damage when they come into contact with the moist environments of a dog's mouth, esophagus, or stomach.

Is my dog swallowing a hearing aid battery a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-urgency medical emergency that requires immediate professional intervention.
  • Punctured or stationary batteries can cause severe corrosive burns, internal bleeding, and tissue death within just a few hours.

How does a photo of the battery help the veterinarian during triage?

  • If safe to do so, take a photo of the battery packaging or an identical spare to help the vet identify its size and chemistry (such as lithium or zinc-air).
  • A photo allows the veterinary team to quickly determine the risk of electrical versus chemical burns.
  • This information helps the clinic prepare the correct treatment plan before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

If a dog has swallowed a hearing aid battery, which are commonly alkaline or lithium-based, there is a risk of gastrointestinal tract corrosive injury and foreign body obstruction. Radiographs may help confirm the diagnosis and location, although some batteries do not show up well. If the battery was swallowed intact without chewing, emesis may be induced for expulsion unless the casing is punctured. For intact disk batteries, administer 20 mL boluses of tap water every 15 minutes to decrease the severity of current-induced esophageal ulceration. Batteries that do not pass through the pylorus within 48 hours of ingestion may require surgical or endoscopic removal. Punctured batteries should be surgically removed to prevent gastric or intestinal ulceration from alkaline gel leakage. Serial radiography is recommended to monitor the battery's location until it is expelled.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3003)

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting if your dog swallowed a battery unless explicitly directed by a veterinarian. If the battery is brought back up, it can cause further corrosive damage to the esophagus or even become lodged.

faqs.question: What are the early signs of battery ingestion?
faqs.answer: You may notice excessive drooling, oral pain, pawing at the mouth, or a blackish residue on the tongue. However, internal damage can occur without any visible outward symptoms, which is why immediate imaging (X-rays) is necessary.

faqs.question: Are 'dead' batteries still dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even a battery that no longer powers a hearing aid often holds enough residual charge to cause significant electrical burns to the internal tissues of a pet.
